w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh w16sdtfl w16du wp14

w:r.w:t: CONFIDENTIAL — For candidate use only. Do not distribute.

Build a fully functional meeting scheduling web application using AI coding tools. This assignment evaluates your ability to leverage AI-assisted development to rapidly build, iterate, and ship a production-quality product. You will replicate the
meeting booking flow shown in the reference screenshots provided.

You must log every single prompt you enter into your AI coding IDE (Cursor, Antigravity, VS Code Copilot, Windsurf, etc.). Prompt logs are a mandatory deliverable and will be reviewed to evaluate your problem-solving approach, prompt engineering skills, and how effectively you decompose tasks for AI assistance.
A meeting scheduling application that replicates the following user flow:
Calendar View: User sees a monthly calendar (March 2026) and selects a date. Available dates are highlighted; weekends and past dates are disabled.
Time Slot Selection: After selecting a date, available 15-minute interval time slots appear (e.g., 16:30, 16:45, 17:00, etc.). A
selector dropdown allows the user to switch
Booking Form: After choosing a time, the user is taken to a form to enter their first name, surname, and email address. The selected date, time, and meeting location (Google Meet) are displayed.
Confirmation Screen: On submission, a confirmation page displays with the meeting details, celebratory graphics, and a message that an invitation email has been sent.
Email Notification: A confirmation email is sent (or simulated) containing meeting details: attendee email, date/time with
, and a Google Meet link. Include Reschedule and Cancel action buttons.
Reference the provided screenshots for exact UI/UX expectations. Your app should match the look and feel as closely as possible, including the
Frontend: React.js or Next.js with TypeScript
Backend: Node.js/Express or Next.js API routes
Database: Any (PostgreSQL, SQLite, MongoDB, or even a JSON file for prototype)
, Resend, or any email service (simulated/test mode is acceptable)
Interactive calendar with date selection (disable past dates and weekends)
Dynamic time slot generation based on availability configuration
selector with automatic time conversion (UTC+05:00 through UTC+07:00 at minimum)
Multi-step booking form with proper validation
Booking confirmation page with meeting summary
Email notification on successful booking (can use test/sandbox email service)
Responsive design that works on desktop and mobile
Proper error handling and loading states
Google Calendar API integration for real availability checking
Reschedule and cancel functionality from confirmation email
Persistent booking storage with an admin view of all bookings
Rate limiting and anti-spam measures on the booking form
This is the most important evaluation criterion.
Your prompt log reveals how you think, debug, and architect solutions. Incomplete or missing logs will result in automatic disqualification.
Maintain a file called PROMPT_LOG.md in the root of your project repository. Every interaction with an AI coding assistant must be recorded in this file with the following format:
- **What I Changed After:** [Any manual edits made to AI output]
- **Why:** [Brief reasoning for the prompt / what you were trying to solve]
What We Evaluate in Your Prompt Log
Breaking features into small, focused prompts
Single prompt: "Build me the whole app"
Follow-up prompts that improve on AI output
Accepting every AI output without review
Using AI to debug with clear error context
Repeatedly pasting same error with no context
Asking about design decisions before coding
No planning prompts, jumping straight to code
Manual edits that show you understand the code
Zero manual modifications to any AI output
GitHub Repository: Public or private repo (add sushil@tese.io as collaborator if private) containing all source code, with a clear README.md with setup instructions.
PROMPT_LOG.md: Complete log of every AI prompt used during development (see format above). This file must be in the repo root.
, Netlify, Railway, or any hosting platform. Include the URL in your README.
Screen Recording (Optional but Recommended): A 3–5 minute Loom video walking through your app and explaining key decisions.
Total: 100 points. Minimum passing score: 60 points.
Well-decomposed, iterative prompts with clear context; manual refinements logged
Decent prompts but some are too broad; logging mostly complete
Vague prompts, missing logs, or single mega-prompts
Core flow works with minor bugs; 3–4 steps complete
Broken flow; major features missing or non-functional
Pixel-close match to reference screenshots; responsive; polished animations
Recognizable design; some visual differences; mostly responsive
Significantly different from reference; broken layout; no mobile support
Clean architecture; TypeScript types; proper error handling; well-organized
Reasonable structure; some typing; basic error handling
Messy code; no types; no error handling; everything in one file
Multiple bonus features; tests; accessibility; creative additions
One bonus feature; basic tests or one extra improvement
No extras attempted beyond minimum requirements
Any AI coding IDE: Cursor, Antigravity, VS Code with Copilot/
Any AI model: GPT-4, Claude, Gemini, or whichever your IDE uses
Referencing documentation, Stack Overflow, and online tutorials
Using starter templates or boilerplate generators (e.g., create-next-app)
Copying an existing meeting scheduler codebase (e.g., cloning Cal.com)
Having another person write code or prompts for you
Using pre-built meeting scheduler SaaS products and just embedding them

You have 48 hours from receiving this assignment. We do not expect you to work the entire 48 hours — a skilled candidate using AI tools effectively should be able to complete this in 4–8 focused hours. The extended window is to accommodate your schedule.
Push all code and PROMPT_LOG.md to your GitHub repository.
Ensure the README.md has clear setup/run instructions (
run dev should ideally be enough).
Email your submission to sushil@tese.io with subject line: "SWE Intern Assessment — [Your Full Name]"
Include in the email: GitHub repo link, live demo URL (if any), and Loom video link (if any).
Deadline: 48 hours from when you received this assignment.
Q: Do I need to build a real email sending system?
A: No. Using a test/sandbox email service (like Ethereal,
, or Resend test mode) is perfectly fine. Console-logging the email content as a fallback is also acceptable, though actually sending emails earns bonus points.
Q: What if my AI IDE doesn't have a built-in way to export prompts?

A: Manually copy each prompt into PROMPT_LOG.md as you go. We recommend keeping the file open in a split editor and logging after each significant AI interaction. Some IDEs like Cursor have conversation history you can reference.
Q: Can I use a different design instead of the
A: No. Part of this assessment is your ability to replicate a specific design from reference screenshots. Match the
scheme, layout, and style as closely as possible.
Q: What if I get stuck?

A: Getting stuck is expected and is part of what we evaluate. We want to see how you use AI to unblock yourself. Log those debugging prompts — they're some of the most valuable entries in your prompt log.
Q: How many prompts should I have in my log?

A: There is no minimum or maximum, but a typical strong submission has 30–80+ logged prompts. Fewer than 15 raises questions about completeness; more than 100 shows thoroughness. Quality matters more than quantity.

w:r.w:t: Good luck! We're excited to see what you build.
If you have any questions, reach out to sushil@tese.io
